prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is the original verbatim passage? The other passages are paraphrased versions of it.

Options:
A. On 3 October 2014, Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper announced that he would put forth a motion to send forces to participate in the coalition for military intervention against ISIL by deploying combat aircraft. On 7 October 2014, the House of Commons approved of sending nine aircraft to join coalition airstrikes against ISIL in Iraq along with 69 special forces advisors to train the Iraqi government's military. The Canadian Armed Forces' contribution to the coalition against ISIL was later dubbed Operation Impact. On 30 March 2015, the House of Commons voted to extend the mission to targets in Syria. No additional forces were announced.In late November 2015, new Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced the withdrawal of Canada's fighter aircraft from the fight against ISIL, but their surveillance and
B. On October 3, 2014, Stephen Harper, who was then the Prime Minister of Canada, declared his intention to propose a motion for the deployment of troops to take part in the military intervention coalition against ISIL, specifically through the use of combat aircraft. Four days later, on October 7, 2014, the House of Commons gave its approval for dispatching nine aircraft to participate in coalition airstrikes targeting ISIL within Iraq, in addition to sending 69 special forces advisors to assist in training the military of the Iraqi government. This involvement of the Canadian Armed Forces in the anti-ISIL coalition was subsequently named Operation Impact. On March 30, 2015, the House of Commons cast votes to prolong the mission, expanding its scope to include targets located in Syria. No further troop deployments were disclosed at that time. Towards the end of November 2015, Justin Trudeau, the newly appointed Prime Minister, made an announcement regarding the removal of Canada's fighter jets from the conflict against ISIL; however, their surveillance and refueling aircraft would continue to provide support.
C. The Canadian Prime Minister, Stephen Harper, announced on 3 October 2014 that he would send forces to participate in the coalition military intervention against ISIL by deploying combat aircraft. On 7 October 2014, the House of Commons approved the deployment of nine aircraft and 69 special forces advisors to train the Iraqi government's military. The Canadian Armed Forces' contribution to the coalition was later called Operation Impact. The House of Commons voted to extend the mission to targets in Syria on 30 March 2015, but no additional forces were announced.

Answer:

options.A: On 3 October 2014, Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper announced that he would put forth a motion to send forces to participate in the coalition for military intervention against ISIL by deploying combat aircraft. On 7 October 2014, the House of Commons approved of sending nine aircraft to join coalition airstrikes against ISIL in Iraq along with 69 special forces advisors to train the Iraqi government's military. The Canadian Armed Forces' contribution to the coalition against ISIL was later dubbed Operation Impact. On 30 March 2015, the House of Commons voted to extend the mission to targets in Syria. No additional forces were announced.In late November 2015, new Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced the withdrawal of Canada's fighter aircraft from the fight against ISIL, but their surveillance and
options.B: On October 3, 2014, Stephen Harper, who was then the Prime Minister of Canada, declared his intention to propose a motion for the deployment of troops to take part in the military intervention coalition against ISIL, specifically through the use of combat aircraft. Four days later, on October 7, 2014, the House of Commons gave its approval for dispatching nine aircraft to participate in coalition airstrikes targeting ISIL within Iraq, in addition to sending 69 special forces advisors to assist in training the military of the Iraqi government. This involvement of the Canadian Armed Forces in the anti-ISIL coalition was subsequently named Operation Impact. On March 30, 2015, the House of Commons cast votes to prolong the mission, expanding its scope to include targets located in Syria. No further troop deployments were disclosed at that time. Towards the end of November 2015, Justin Trudeau, the newly appointed Prime Minister, made an announcement regarding the removal of Canada's fighter jets from the conflict against ISIL; however, their surveillance and refueling aircraft would continue to provide support.
options.C: The Canadian Prime Minister, Stephen Harper, announced on 3 October 2014 that he would send forces to participate in the coalition military intervention against ISIL by deploying combat aircraft. On 7 October 2014, the House of Commons approved the deployment of nine aircraft and 69 special forces advisors to train the Iraqi government's military. The Canadian Armed Forces' contribution to the coalition was later called Operation Impact. The House of Commons voted to extend the mission to targets in Syria on 30 March 2015, but no additional forces were announced.
